text.tspan: server = hash(key) % N
+ trivial to compute
+ perfectly balanced when N stable
u2212 nearly every key moves when N changes
u2212 cannot add/remove nodes gracefully
USE: never in production; pedagogical only
place servers on a ring (vnodes); key goes to next server clockwise
+ adding/removing N nodes moves O(K/N) keys
+ supports weighted servers via vnode count
u2212 vnodes use memory for routing tables
u2212 still hotspot risk on sequential keys (mitigated by salting)
USE: caches (Memcached client-side), DynamoDB, Cassandra, Riak
for each server, compute hash(key, server); pick highest
+ same key-movement properties as consistent hashing
+ NO vnodes / no ring data structure
+ very even distribution (no hotspots)
u2212 O(N) per lookup (vs O(log N) for ring); fine for moderate N
USE: GitHub Spokes, some video/CDN systems, gRPC client load balancing
partition by key range (e.g. user-id 1..1000 -> shard 0, 1001..2000 -> shard 1)
+ supports efficient RANGE QUERIES (scan a contiguous range)
+ data locality u2014 related keys live together
u2212 HOTSPOT RISK: hot range (e.g. recent timestamps) overwhelms one shard
u2212 requires manual or automatic split/merge to rebalance
USE: HBase, Bigtable, Spanner, CockroachDB, MongoDB (with shard key)
encode (lat, lon) into a string where prefix = nearby region
e.g. 9q8yyk = San Francisco, 9q8yyu = a few blocks away
+ supports proximity queries via prefix matching
+ uniform 2D grid; works with range partitioning
u2212 NOT uniform load (cities are hotspots)
u2212 boundary cases need neighbor lookups
USE: Uber (H3 is similar), Lyft, restaurant finders, geo-spatial DBs
